title: BBC 6 Minute English
description: Learn English in just 6 minutes with BBC topics — news, culture, science and more

episodes.title: Is Remote Work Here to Stay?
episodes.description: Discuss the future of working from home and its impact on cities and employees

transcript.text: Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English. I'm Neil.
And I'm Sam. Today we're talking about remote work — working from home instead of in an office.
The pandemic changed everything for millions of workers around the world.
But now that offices are open again, many companies are asking people to come back.
Neil, do you prefer working from home or the office?
That's a great question. I think flexibility is key — different people work better in different environments.
Our word of the day is 'hybrid' — meaning a mix of two things. A hybrid work model combines home and office working.
Studies show that employees with flexible arrangements are often more productive.
However, some managers worry about collaboration — working together closely as a team.
They argue that creativity and innovation happen best when people are face to face.
On the other hand, remote workers report better work-life balance and less commuting stress.
A commute is the journey from home to work — often by train, bus or car.
Major tech companies like Apple and Google now require employees to come in at least three days a week.
This is causing controversy — some workers are resigning rather than return to the office full-time.
Today's question: What percentage of workers globally now work hybrid? Is it 30%, 40% or 60%?
I'll say 40% — that feels about right to me.
The answer is actually 60% according to recent surveys. Remote and hybrid work is very widespread.
The word 'widespread' means existing or happening in many places.
Let's look at some useful vocabulary from today's topic.
Flexibility — the ability to adapt to different situations or needs.
Productivity — the efficiency with which someone completes work.
Collaboration — working jointly with others to produce something.
Work-life balance — having enough time for both work and personal activities.
And that's 6 Minute English. See you next time!

episodes.title: The Science of Sleep
episodes.description: Explore why sleep is so important for health and how much we really need

transcript.text: Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English. I'm Neil.
And I'm Beth. Today we're exploring the science of sleep.
Most adults need between 7 and 9 hours of sleep per night.
But in our busy modern world, many people get far less.
Today's question: What percentage of adults report regular sleep problems? Is it 20%, 40% or 60%?
Hmm, I'll guess 40%.
The answer is about 40% — sleep deprivation is a global health crisis.
Sleep deprivation means not getting enough sleep — and it has serious consequences.
These include poor concentration, weakened immunity, and higher risk of heart disease.
Our word today is 'circadian' — from the Latin for 'about a day'. Your circadian rhythm is your body's natural 24-hour clock.
This clock tells your body when to feel sleepy and when to be alert.
Blue light from phones and screens disrupts this rhythm, making it harder to fall asleep.
Experts recommend turning off screens at least an hour before bed.
During sleep, your brain processes memories and your body repairs itself.
There are different stages of sleep — including REM, which stands for Rapid Eye Movement.
REM sleep is when most dreaming occurs and is essential for memory and creativity.
Let's review today's vocabulary: deprivation, circadian rhythm, immunity, REM sleep.
And that is 6 Minute English for today. Sleep well and see you soon!

episodes.title: Artificial Intelligence in Everyday Life
episodes.description: How AI is changing the way we live, work, and make decisions

transcript.text: Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English. I'm Sam.
And I'm Neil. Today, we're discussing artificial intelligence — or AI.
AI refers to computer systems that can perform tasks normally requiring human intelligence.
From facial recognition to recommendation algorithms, AI is everywhere.
Our question today: In what year did a computer first beat a world chess champion?
I'll guess 1997.
Correct! In 1997, IBM's Deep Blue defeated Garry Kasparov, marking a milestone for AI.
A milestone is an important event or achievement in a development process.
Today, AI can write essays, compose music, and even diagnose medical conditions.
But there are concerns too — about bias, job displacement and privacy.
Bias in AI means the system reflects and reinforces human prejudices from its training data.
Job displacement refers to workers losing their jobs because machines can do them instead.
Not everyone sees AI as a threat though. Many argue it will create new kinds of jobs.
The key is upskilling — learning new skills to adapt to a changing job market.
Today's vocabulary: milestone, bias, displacement, upskilling, algorithm.
Thanks for listening to 6 Minute English. See you next time!

episodes.title: The Benefits of Learning a Second Language
episodes.description: Discover how bilingualism boosts brain health and opens doors

transcript.text: Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English. I'm Beth.
And I'm Neil. Today we're asking: does learning a second language make you smarter?
Today's question: How many languages does the average European citizen speak? Is it 1, 2 or 3?
I'd say 2 — most Europeans I've met speak at least some English as well as their native tongue.
That's correct! The average EU citizen speaks 2 languages. Some speak 3 or more.
Our key word today is bilingual — someone who speaks two languages fluently.
Researchers have found that bilingual people switch between tasks more efficiently than monolinguals.
A monolingual is someone who speaks only one language. The prefix mono- means one.
This mental agility comes from constantly managing two language systems in the brain.
Agility means the ability to move or think quickly and easily.
Studies also show bilingualism can delay the onset of dementia by several years.
Onset means the beginning of something, often something unpleasant.
Beyond health, second languages open up career opportunities and cultural understanding.
Today's vocabulary: bilingual, monolingual, agility, onset, fluency, immersion.
Thanks for joining us. Keep practising — see you next time!

episodes.title: Plastic Pollution in Our Oceans
episodes.description: The alarming reality of plastic waste and what can be done

transcript.text: Welcome to 6 Minute English. I'm Sam, and with me is Neil.
Today's topic is plastic pollution — a problem that threatens our oceans and wildlife.
Today's question: How many tonnes of plastic enter the ocean every year? 1 million, 8 million or 20 million?
I'll guess 8 million. That sounds horrifying but plausible.
You're right — around 8 million tonnes of plastic enter the ocean annually.
Once in the ocean, plastic breaks down into microplastics — tiny particles less than 5 millimetres in size.
Microplastics have been found in fish, drinking water, and even human blood.
Our word today is pervasive — meaning present everywhere, very widespread.
Plastic pollution is pervasive — it reaches from Arctic ice to deep ocean trenches.
Some countries have introduced single-use plastic bans to tackle the crisis.
Single-use means something designed to be used once and then thrown away.
However, critics say bans alone are insufficient without better waste management globally.
Insufficient means not enough — the opposite of sufficient.
Today's vocabulary: microplastics, pervasive, single-use, sufficient, pollution, marine.
That's 6 Minute English. Thank you for listening!

episodes.title: Mental Health at Work
episodes.description: How companies are tackling stress, burnout and wellbeing in the workplace

transcript.text: Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English. I'm Neil and I'm joined by Beth.
Today we're talking about mental health at work — an increasingly important topic.
Our question: What percentage of sick days in the UK are caused by mental health issues? 25%, 40% or 57%?
I'd guess 40% — mental health problems are very common.
The answer is actually 57% — mental health issues are the leading cause of sick leave in the UK.
Today's key term is burnout — a state of physical and emotional exhaustion caused by prolonged stress.
Signs of burnout include constant fatigue, cynicism about work, and reduced performance.
Cynicism means a general distrust or negative view — being cynical means always expecting the worst.
Many forward-thinking employers are now introducing wellbeing programmes.
These might include mental health days, access to therapists, and flexible working.
Flexible working allows employees to choose when and where they work within agreed boundaries.
Research shows that workplaces with strong wellbeing cultures have lower turnover — meaning fewer people leave.
Today's vocabulary: burnout, cynicism, wellbeing, flexibility, turnover, resilience.
Join us again next time for more 6 Minute English!

episodes.title: The Rise of Veganism
episodes.description: Why more people than ever are choosing a plant-based lifestyle

transcript.text: Welcome to 6 Minute English. I'm Sam and today I'm with Neil.
We're discussing veganism — a lifestyle that avoids all animal products.
Today's question: How much has the number of vegans in the UK grown in the last decade? 200%, 350% or 600%?
I'll say 350% — it feels like vegan options are everywhere now.
The answer is over 600% — veganism has grown dramatically in the UK.
Our word today is plant-based — food or products made entirely from plants, with nothing from animals.
Vegans avoid meat, fish, dairy, eggs, and often honey and leather too.
Reasons for going vegan include environmental concerns, animal welfare, and health benefits.
Animal welfare refers to how well animals are treated — their physical and mental wellbeing.
Producing one kilogram of beef generates 20 times more greenhouse gas than tofu.
However, critics say plant-based diets require careful planning to avoid nutritional deficiencies.
A deficiency is when you don't have enough of something your body needs — like vitamin B12 or iron.
Today's vocabulary: veganism, plant-based, welfare, emissions, deficiency, sustainable.
That's 6 Minute English for today. Thanks for listening!

episodes.title: Space Exploration Today
episodes.description: From Mars missions to private spaceflight — the new era of space exploration

transcript.text: Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English. I'm Beth and I'm with Sam.
We're discussing what many call the new space race — but this time with private companies involved.
Today's question: In what year did SpaceX first successfully land a reusable rocket booster? 2013, 2015 or 2018?
I'll guess 2015 — that sounds about right.
Correct! In 2015 SpaceX landed a Falcon 9 booster for the first time, transforming space economics.
A reusable rocket can be flown multiple times, dramatically cutting the cost of reaching space.
Our word today is unprecedented — meaning never done or seen before. That landing was truly unprecedented.
NASA's Artemis programme aims to return humans to the Moon by the mid-2020s.
Ultimately, Mars is the destination — a crewed mission may happen within the next 20 years.
Crewed means carrying human astronauts, as opposed to robotic or unmanned missions.
Critics question whether the enormous cost is justified when so many problems remain on Earth.
Proponents argue space technology generates innovations that benefit everyone — from GPS to memory foam.
Today's vocabulary: reusable, unprecedented, crewed, proponents, innovation, trajectory.
That's 6 Minute English. We'll be back next week!
